Project: Investigating New Orleans 311 Data¶

Student Name¶

Syeda Mah Noor Asad

Webpage¶

https://syedamahnoorasad.github.io/Investigating-Nola-311-Data/

In [ ]:

Dataset Choice¶

I am using NOLA 311 Calls/Requests Dataset and Chicago 311 Data from 2020-2025. The datasets consist of records produced by the residents of New Orleans who submit their non-emergency problems to the city governments. The datasets are available publicly at: https://data.nola.gov/City-Administration/311-OPCD-Calls-2012-Present-/2jgv-pqrq/data_preview and https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/311-service-requests. I downloaded the data in CSV format which I later import into a dataframe using pandas.

I am picking these datasets due to these reasons:

  1. One dataset relates to New Orleans so it hits close to home.
  2. Both datasets are large and at the time of downloading had over 900,000 (NO) and 3 million (Chicago) records.
  3. Both datasets are generally fairly organized but have some issues with consistency, tidiness, and missing fields which make them great candidates for pre-processing.
  4. Within this data there are several different categories of issues reported. Such as entries pertaining to sewerage, parking, accessibility, electricity and so on. These sub-categories can be relevant and interesting.
  5. It is real, and open-government data that can lead to practical insights depicting real world trends.
  6. My own Phd research is based around civic data and I think this is very in line and relevant to that. It will be helpful for me to investigate a data like this.

Project Plan¶

In this project, I compare 311 service request data from New Orleans and Chicago between 2020 and 2025. Rather than focusing only on accessibility issues in one city, I use both datasets to study how request volume, category mix, data completeness, and service performance differ across two urban contexts. The analysis centers on cleaning and standardizing the records, then using exploratory analysis to understand what residents report, when they report it, and how quickly those requests are resolved.

Some of the main questions I carried out in this project are:

1. Dataset Quality and Comparability¶

1.1. How large are the two datasets, and how do they compare once population size is taken into account?

1.2. How complete are key fields such as location, agency, request status, and closure date in each city?

2. Temporal and Request Pattern Analysis¶

2.1. How does 311 request activity change across months, years, and days of the week in New Orleans and Chicago?

2.2. Are seasonal peaks or major events, such as summer surges or hurricanes, visible in the request volume data?

2.3. What are the most common request categories in each city, and what do those differences suggest about local civic concerns?

3. Service Performance and Spatial Equity¶

3.1. How long do requests take to close in each city, and how is that time distributed across records?

3.2. Do some New Orleans council districts or Chicago precincts appear to resolve requests more efficiently than others?

3.3. Which request categories have the highest median and mean resolution times, and what might that imply about priority and service burden?

Workflow¶

Import first dataset: New Orleans 311 Data¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid')
In [62]:
## Import data from the dataset csv file

nola_311 = pd.read_csv('311_OPCD_Calls_2012-Present.csv')

## Display the first few rows of the dataset to understand its structure
nola_311.head()

Tidying the New Orleans Data¶

In [63]:
### dropping redundant columns
nola_311.drop(columns=['Location'], inplace=True)

In [64]:
### Fixing column names 

### stripped leading and white space, converted to lowercase, replaced spaces with underscores
nola_311.columns = nola_311.columns.str.strip().str.lower().str.replace(' ', '_')

### rename column 
nola_311.rename(columns={'service_request_#': 'request_number'}, inplace=True)
nola_311.rename(columns={'rowid': 'row_id'}, inplace=True)
nola_311.rename(columns={'x': 'projected_x', 'y': 'projected_y'}, inplace=True)


nola_311.head()

In [67]:
### convert columns to appropriate data types
nola_311['request_number'] = nola_311['request_number'].astype(str)
nola_311['row_id'] = nola_311['row_id'].str.replace(',', '').astype(int)
nola_311['projected_x'] = nola_311['projected_x'].str.replace(',', '').astype(float)
nola_311['projected_y'] = nola_311['projected_y'].str.replace(',', '').astype(float)
nola_311['date_modified'] = pd.to_datetime(nola_311['date_modified'])
nola_311['date_created'] = pd.to_datetime(nola_311['date_created'])
nola_311['date_closed'] = pd.to_datetime(nola_311['date_closed'])

nola_311.dtypes

In [68]:
### subset New Orleans data from January 1, 2020 through December 31, 2025
nola_start_date = pd.Timestamp('2020-01-01')
nola_end_date = pd.Timestamp('2025-12-31 23:59:59')

nola_311 = nola_311[
    nola_311['date_created'].between(nola_start_date, nola_end_date)
].copy()

nola_311.head()

In [69]:
### checking for missing values in the dataset
nola_311.isnull().sum()

### replace nan with 'Unknown' in the object type columns
object_columns = nola_311.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns
nola_311[object_columns] = nola_311[object_columns].fillna('Unknown')

### replace projected_x and projected_y with 0 for missing values
nola_311['projected_x'] = nola_311['projected_x'].fillna(0)
nola_311['projected_y'] = nola_311['projected_y'].fillna(0)
In [71]:
nola_311.shape
Out[71]:
(566555, 18)

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Request Types and distribution¶

Important for Inquiry 1.1

In [12]:
### unqiue values in request_type column
request_types = nola_311['request_type'].unique()
print(f"Unique request types: {request_types}")

Unique request types: ["Mayor's Request" 'Tax and Revenue' 'Traffic Safety' 'Trash/Recycling'
 'Roads and Streets' 'Streetlights' 'Traffic Signals' 'Safety & Permits'
 'Utility Poles' 'Street and Traffic Signs' 'Parks & Parkways'
 'General Services' 'Health Related Request' 'EMS' 'Abandoned Vehicles'
 'Roads/Drainage' 'Property Maintenance'
 'Traffic Signals/Signs/Striping/Streetlights' 'Health Related Questions'
 'Mosquito, Termite & Rodent Control' 'Law Enforcement Services'
 'Alley Ways' 'COVID-19' 'Taxi Issues' 'Drainage' 'Road Striping'
 'City-Owned Cemeteries' 'Healthy Homes'
 'New Orleans Recreation Development Commission (NORDC)'
 'Graffiti Removal' 'Family Reunification'
 'Hurricane Francine Tree-Related Issues or Emergencies\t'
 'Christmas Tree Recycle Pick Up' 'Unknown' 'Hard Rock Hotel Collapse']
In [13]:
### grouby request type and count number of requests for each type
request_type_counts = nola_311.groupby('request_type').size()

### sort the request type counts in descending order and display the top 10 request types
request_type_counts.sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
Out[13]:
request_type
Trash/Recycling             307942
Abandoned Vehicles           46874
Property Maintenance         42158
Streetlights                 31861
Roads and Streets            26191
Drainage                     19303
Parks & Parkways             16250
Roads/Drainage               14651
Street and Traffic Signs      9604
Safety & Permits              8269
dtype: int64
In [14]:
### bar plot of top 10 request types
top_request_types = request_type_counts.sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=top_request_types.values, y=top_request_types.index, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Top 10 Request Types in NOLA 311 Dataset')
plt.xlabel('Number of Requests')
plt.ylabel('Request Type')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

/var/folders/r0/6m_3dcrs53qf0lfygy7ffmwm0000gn/T/ipykernel_9110/1988747233.py:4: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `y` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=top_request_types.values, y=top_request_types.index, palette='viridis')
Time distribution for requests¶

Important for inquiry 1.2 to find the average time distribution and potential discrepencies between types and gerographical reasons.

In [15]:
### groupby request type and calculate average time to close each request type in days
nola_311['time_to_close'] = (nola_311['date_closed'] - nola_311['date_created']).dt.total_seconds() / 3600 / 24
avg_time_to_close = nola_311.groupby('request_type')['time_to_close'].mean().sort_values(ascending=True).head(10)
print(avg_time_to_close)
  

request_type
Hurricane Francine Tree-Related Issues or Emergencies\t     12.702100
Family Reunification                                        16.703810
Healthy Homes                                               17.540577
General Services                                            22.621554
Utility Poles                                               28.113462
City-Owned Cemeteries                                       31.894843
Health Related Questions                                    51.463029
Mayor's Request                                             63.636145
EMS                                                         82.545524
Traffic Signals/Signs/Striping/Streetlights                110.337250
Name: time_to_close, dtype: float64
In [53]:
### check for cases where date_closed is before date_created and status is 'Closed'
invalid_dates = nola_311[
    (nola_311['date_closed'] < nola_311['date_created']) &
    (nola_311['status'] == 'Closed')
]
### remove records with invalid dates
nola_311 = nola_311.drop(invalid_dates.index)
print (f"Number of records after removing invalid dates: {len(nola_311)}")

Number of records after removing invalid dates: 397452
In [55]:
### average time to close a request by request type (in hours) in ascending order
print("Request types with lowest average time to close a request (in days):")
nola_311.groupby('request_type')['time_to_close'].mean().sort_values(ascending=True).head(10)

Request types with lowest average time to close a request (in days):

In [56]:
### new top 10 common requests with their average time to close in days
top10 = nola_311['request_type'].value_counts().head(10).index

avg_time_top10 = (
    nola_311[nola_311['request_type'].isin(top10)]
    .groupby('request_type')['time_to_close']
    .mean()
    .reindex(top10)
)

### plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=avg_time_top10.values, y=avg_time_top10.index, palette='magma')
plt.title('Average Time to Close Top 10 Request Types (in Days)')
plt.xlabel('Average Time to Close (Days)')
plt.ylabel('Request Type')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

/var/folders/r0/6m_3dcrs53qf0lfygy7ffmwm0000gn/T/ipykernel_9110/2586559578.py:13: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `y` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=avg_time_top10.values, y=avg_time_top10.index, palette='magma')
In [57]:
### distribution of time to close requests
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.histplot(nola_311['time_to_close'], bins=300, kde=True, color='blue')
plt.title('Distribution of Time to Close Requests (in Days)')
plt.xlabel('Time to Close (Days)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.xlim(0, 200)
plt.xticks(range(0, 300, 20))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [58]:
### scatter plot of average time to close requests by request type
avg_time_by_type = nola_311.groupby('request_type')['time_to_close'].mean().reset_index()
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.scatterplot(data=avg_time_by_type, x='request_type', y='time_to_close', color='red')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title('Average Time to Close Requests by Request Type')
plt.xlabel('Request Type')
plt.ylabel('Average Time to Close (Days)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

/var/folders/r0/6m_3dcrs53qf0lfygy7ffmwm0000gn/T/ipykernel_9110/3629302438.py:9: UserWarning: Glyph 9 (	) missing from font(s) Arial.
  plt.tight_layout()
/Users/syedamahnoorasad/Library/Python/3.12/lib/python/site-packages/IPython/core/pylabtools.py:170: UserWarning: Glyph 9 (	) missing from font(s) Arial.
  fig.canvas.print_figure(bytes_io, **kw)

This visualization helps us compare all the request types. We can see that Hard Rock hotel colapse and Covid-19 requets had the highest resolution times.

Geographical distribution¶

Important for inquiry 2.2

In [59]:
### districts with highest number of requests
district_counts = nola_311['council_district'].value_counts()
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=district_counts.values, y=district_counts.index, palette='coolwarm')
plt.title('Districts by Number of Requests')
plt.xlabel('Number of Requests')
plt.ylabel('District')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

/var/folders/r0/6m_3dcrs53qf0lfygy7ffmwm0000gn/T/ipykernel_9110/1460902740.py:4: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `y` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=district_counts.values, y=district_counts.index, palette='coolwarm')

The number of requests per district is fairly even for top 3 districts. More investigation would need to go into determining whether the difference between these number of requests is significant or not.

In [60]:
### Average resolution time by district
avg_time_by_district = nola_311.groupby('council_district')['time_to_close'].mean().reset_index()
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=avg_time_by_district['time_to_close'], y=avg_time_by_district['council_district'], palette='viridis')
plt.title('Average Time to Close Requests by District')
plt.xlabel('Average Time to Close (Days)')
plt.ylabel('District')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

/var/folders/r0/6m_3dcrs53qf0lfygy7ffmwm0000gn/T/ipykernel_9110/2122413471.py:4: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `y` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=avg_time_by_district['time_to_close'], y=avg_time_by_district['council_district'], palette='viridis')

I think it is interesting that District D has significantly higher average resolution time. This would require more investigation later.

Import Second Dataset: Chicago 311 Data¶

In [23]:
## Import data from the dataset csv file

chunks = []

for chunk in pd.read_csv(
    '311_Service_Requests.csv',
    low_memory=False,
    chunksize=500000
):
    chunk['CREATED_DATE'] = pd.to_datetime(
        chunk['CREATED_DATE'],
        format='%m/%d/%Y %I:%M:%S %p',
        errors='coerce'
    )
    
    chunk = chunk.loc[
        chunk['CREATED_DATE'].between('2020-01-01', '2025-12-31 23:59:59')
    ]
    chunks.append(chunk)

DATA = pd.concat(chunks, ignore_index=True)
In [24]:
chi_311 = DATA.copy()

## Display the first few rows of the dataset to understand its structure
chi_311.head()
Out[24]:
SR_NUMBER SR_TYPE SR_SHORT_CODE CREATED_DEPARTMENT OWNER_DEPARTMENT STATUS ORIGIN CREATED_DATE LAST_MODIFIED_DATE CLOSED_DATE ... PRECINCT SANITATION_DIVISION_DAYS CREATED_HOUR CREATED_DAY_OF_WEEK CREATED_MONTH X_COORDINATE Y_COORDINATE LATITUDE LONGITUDE LOCATION

2 SR21-00857385 Garbage Cart Maintenance SIE NaN Streets and Sanitation Completed Internet 2021-05-21 13:27:26 06/28/2021 09:15:43 AM 06/28/2021 09:15:43 AM ... 40.0 NaN 13 6 5 1.165578e+06 1.854515e+06 41.756385 -87.668763 (41.75638479819737, -87.66876330253068)
3 SR20-04407621 Traffic Signal Out Complaint SFB 311 City Services CDOT - Department of Transportation Canceled Phone Call 2020-06-22 16:32:39 06/22/2020 04:35:28 PM 06/22/2020 04:35:28 PM ... 2.0 NaN 16 2 6 1.162045e+06 1.841573e+06 41.720945 -87.682070 (41.72094524289129, -87.68207046358089)
4 SR22-01315113 Garbage Cart Maintenance SIE NaN Streets and Sanitation Completed Internet 2022-07-26 17:48:02 09/27/2022 09:40:19 AM 09/27/2022 09:40:19 AM ... 18.0 NaN 17 3 7 1.139717e+06 1.905056e+06 41.895585 -87.762308 (41.89558499575442, -87.76230794313283)

5 rows × 39 columns

Tidying the Chicago Data¶

In [36]:
### rename Chicago columns to match equivalent New Orleans columns
chi_to_nola_column_map = {
    'SR_NUMBER': 'request_number',
    'SR_TYPE': 'request_reason',
    'CREATED_DATE': 'date_created',
    'LAST_MODIFIED_DATE': 'date_modified',
    'CLOSED_DATE': 'date_closed',
    'STATUS': 'request_status',
    'OWNER_DEPARTMENT': 'responsible_agency',
    'STREET_ADDRESS': 'address',
    'LONGITUDE': 'longitude',
    'LATITUDE': 'latitude',
    'ORIGIN': 'origin',
        "PRECINCT": "precinct"
}

# Keep non-equivalent Chicago columns unchanged.
chi_311 = chi_311.rename(columns=chi_to_nola_column_map)

chi_311.columns
Out[36]:
Index(['request_number', 'request_reason', 'origin', 'date_created',
       'date_modified', 'date_closed', 'request_status', 'responsible_agency',
       'address', 'longitude', 'latitude', 'precinct'],
      dtype='object')
In [37]:
### convert Chicago columns to better data types
string_columns = [
    'request_number', 'request_reason', 'request_status',
    'responsible_agency', 'origin', 'address', "precinct"
]

for col in string_columns:
    chi_311[col] = chi_311[col].astype('string')

date_format = '%m/%d/%Y %I:%M:%S %p'

chi_311['date_created'] = pd.to_datetime(chi_311['date_created'], format=date_format, errors='coerce')
chi_311['date_modified'] = pd.to_datetime(chi_311['date_modified'], format=date_format, errors='coerce')
chi_311['date_closed'] = pd.to_datetime(chi_311['date_closed'], format=date_format, errors='coerce')



numeric_columns = [
'latitude', 'longitude'
]

for col in numeric_columns:
    chi_311[col] = pd.to_numeric(chi_311[col], errors='coerce')

chi_311['request_status'] = chi_311['request_status'].replace({'Closed': 'Completed'}).astype('string')

chi_311.dtypes
Out[37]:
request_number        string[python]
request_reason        string[python]
origin                string[python]
date_created          datetime64[ns]
date_modified         datetime64[ns]
date_closed           datetime64[ns]
request_status        string[python]
responsible_agency    string[python]
address               string[python]
longitude                    float64
latitude                     float64
precinct              string[python]
dtype: object
In [38]:
chi_311

### remove columns that are not in both datasets except precinct and origin in Chicago dataset
keep_columns = ['request_number', 'request_reason', 'origin', 'date_created', 'date_modified', 'date_closed', 'request_status', 'responsible_agency', 'address', 'longitude', 'latitude', "precinct"]
chi_311 = chi_311[keep_columns]
In [39]:
chi_311.to_csv('chi_311_clean.csv', index=False)
In [40]:
chi_311['date_created'].min()
Out[40]:
Timestamp('2020-01-01 00:00:15')
In [41]:
chi_status_proportions = (
    chi_311['request_status']
    .value_counts()
    .rename_axis('request_status')
    .reset_index(name='count')
)

chi_status_proportions['proportion'] = (
    chi_status_proportions['count'] / chi_status_proportions['count'].sum()
)

chi_status_proportions
Out[41]:
request_status count proportion
0 Completed 11026236 0.974971
1 Canceled 151148 0.013365
2 Open 131912 0.011664

General Dataset Comparison in Terms of Size¶

In [47]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
ax = sns.barplot(data=chi_status_proportions, x='request_status', y='proportion', hue='request_status', palette='Set2', legend=False)

for bar in ax.patches:
    height = bar.get_height()
    ax.annotate(f'{height:.1%}',
                (bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, height),
                ha='center', va='bottom', xytext=(0, 5), textcoords='offset points')

plt.title('Proportion of Chicago Request Statuses')
plt.xlabel('Request Status')
plt.ylabel('Proportion')
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [70]:
### Proportion bar plot of pending vs closed requests

nola_status_proportions = (
    nola_311['request_status']
    .fillna('Unknown')
    .astype(str)
    .str.strip()
    .value_counts(normalize=True)
    .reindex([ 'Closed', 'Pending'], fill_value=0)
    .rename_axis('request_status')
    .reset_index(name='proportion')
)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
ax = sns.barplot(data=nola_status_proportions, x='request_status', y='proportion', hue='request_status', palette='Set2', legend=False)

for bar in ax.patches:
    height = bar.get_height()
    ax.annotate(f'{height:.1%}',
                (bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, height),
                ha='center', va='bottom', xytext=(0, 5), textcoords='offset points')

plt.title('Proportion of Pending and Closed Requests in New Orleans')
plt.xlabel('Request Status')
plt.ylabel('Proportion')
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [49]:
report_counts = pd.DataFrame({
    'city': ['New Orleans', 'Chicago'],
    'report_count': [len(nola_311), len(chi_311)]
})

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
ax = sns.barplot(data=report_counts, x='city', y='report_count', hue='city', palette='Set2', legend=False)

for bar in ax.patches:
    height = int(bar.get_height())
    ax.annotate(f'{height:,}',
                (bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height()),
                ha='center', va='bottom', xytext=(0, 5), textcoords='offset points')

plt.title('Number of 311 Reports: New Orleans vs Chicago')
plt.xlabel('City')
plt.ylabel('Number of Reports')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [50]:
nola_pop = 384130 ### april 2024
chi_pop = 2748331 ### april 2024


### average number of complaints per 1000 residents  
complaints_per_1000 = pd.DataFrame({
    'city': ['New Orleans', 'Chicago'],
    'complaints_per_1000': [
        len(nola_311) / nola_pop * 1000,
        len(chi_311) / chi_pop * 1000
    ]
})

complaints_per_1000

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
ax = sns.barplot(data=complaints_per_1000, x='city', y='complaints_per_1000', hue='city', palette='Set2', legend=False)

for bar in ax.patches:
    height = bar.get_height()
    ax.annotate(f'{height:.2f}',
                (bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, height),
                ha='center', va='bottom', xytext=(0, 5), textcoords='offset points')

plt.title('311 Complaints per 1,000 Residents')
plt.xlabel('City')
plt.ylabel('Complaints per 1,000 Residents')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Data Completeness¶

Volume & Baseline Comparison¶

The population of New Orleans was 384,139 and that of Chicago was 2,748,331 as of April 2024 [1],[2].¶

In [ ]:
nola_pop = 384130  # April 2024
chi_pop = 2748331  # April 2024

### Taken from https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/neworleanscitylouisiana/PST040224#PST040224 and https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/chicagocityillinois/PST045224

volume_baseline = pd.DataFrame({
    'city': ['New Orleans', 'Chicago'],
    'total_complaints': [len(nola_311), len(chi_311)],
    'population': [nola_pop, chi_pop]
})

volume_baseline['complaints_per_1000'] = (
    volume_baseline['total_complaints'] / (volume_baseline['population'] / 1000)
)

volume_baseline

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
ax = sns.barplot(data=volume_baseline, x='city', y='complaints_per_1000', hue='city', palette='Set2', legend=False)

for bar in ax.patches:
    height = bar.get_height()
    ax.annotate(f'{height:,.1f}',
                (bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, height),
                ha='center', va='bottom', xytext=(0, 5), textcoords='offset points')

plt.title('311 Complaint Volume per 1,000 Residents')
plt.xlabel('City')
plt.ylabel('Complaints per 1,000 Residents')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Data Completeness Audit¶

In [74]:
nola_audit_cols = ['date_closed', 'address', 'responsible_agency','request_reason', 'request_status', 'longitude', 'latitude']
chi_audit_cols = ['date_closed', 'address', 'responsible_agency', 'request_reason', 'request_status', 'longitude', 'latitude']

completeness_audit = pd.DataFrame({
    'New Orleans': nola_311[nola_audit_cols].isna().mean() * 100,
    'Chicago': chi_311[chi_audit_cols].isna().mean() * 100
}).rename_axis('column').reset_index()

completeness_audit

completeness_heatmap = completeness_audit.set_index('column').T

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
sns.heatmap(completeness_heatmap, annot=True, fmt='.1f', cmap='Reds', cbar_kws={'label': '% Missing'})
plt.title('Missingness Audit Across Key Columns')
plt.xlabel('Column')
plt.ylabel('City')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Request Status Proportions¶

In [77]:
status_order_nola = ['Closed', 'Pending', 'Canceled']
status_order_chi = ['Closed', 'Open', 'Canceled']

nola_status_compare = (
    nola_311['request_status']
    .fillna('Unknown')
    .astype(str)
    .str.strip()
    .replace({'Completed': 'Closed'})
    .value_counts(normalize=True)
    .reindex(status_order_nola, fill_value=0)
    .rename_axis('request_status')
    .reset_index(name='proportion')
)
nola_status_compare['city'] = 'New Orleans'

chi_status_compare = (
    chi_311['request_status']
    .fillna('Unknown')
    .astype(str)
    .str.strip()
    .replace({'Completed': 'Closed'})
    .value_counts(normalize=True)
    .reindex(status_order_chi, fill_value=0)
    .rename_axis('request_status')
    .reset_index(name='proportion')
)
chi_status_compare['city'] = 'Chicago'

status_proportions_compare = pd.concat([nola_status_compare, chi_status_compare], ignore_index=True)
status_proportions_compare

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6), sharey=True)

for ax, city in zip(axes, ['New Orleans', 'Chicago']):
    city_data = status_proportions_compare[status_proportions_compare['city'] == city]
    sns.barplot(data=city_data, x='request_status', y='proportion', hue='request_status', palette='Set2', legend=False, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(f'{city} Request Status Proportions')
    ax.set_xlabel('Request Status')
    ax.set_ylabel('Proportion')
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
    for bar in ax.patches:
        height = bar.get_height()
        ax.annotate(f'{height:.1%}',
                    (bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, height),
                    ha='center', va='bottom', xytext=(0, 5), textcoords='offset points')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Temporal Analysis¶

Temporal analysis can show really interesting things in the data the sort we are dealing with. Let's see how the request volume fluctuates over time.¶

Request Volume Trends Across the Months and Years (2020-2025)¶

In [82]:
nola_monthly_trend = (
    nola_311[nola_311['date_created'].between('2020-01-01', '2025-12-31 23:59:59')]
    .assign(month=lambda df: df['date_created'].dt.to_period('M').dt.to_timestamp())
    .groupby('month')
    .size()
    .reset_index(name='report_count')
)
nola_monthly_trend['city'] = 'New Orleans'

chi_monthly_trend = (
    chi_311[chi_311['date_created'].between('2020-01-01', '2025-12-31 23:59:59')]
    .assign(month=lambda df: df['date_created'].dt.to_period('M').dt.to_timestamp())
    .groupby('month')
    .size()
    .reset_index(name='report_count')
)
chi_monthly_trend['city'] = 'Chicago'

monthly_volume_trends = pd.concat([nola_monthly_trend, chi_monthly_trend], ignore_index=True)
monthly_volume_trends.head()


import matplotlib.dates as mdates

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 10), sharex=True)

colors = {'New Orleans': '#2a9d8f', 'Chicago': '#e76f51'}

for ax, city in zip(axes, ['New Orleans', 'Chicago']):
    city_data = monthly_volume_trends[monthly_volume_trends['city'] == city]
    sns.lineplot(
        data=city_data,
        x='month',
        y='report_count',
        color=colors[city],
        linewidth=2,
        ax=ax
    )
    ax.set_title(f'{city} Monthly 311 Request Volume (2020-2025)')
    ax.set_ylabel('Number of Reports')
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator(interval=3))
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelbottom=True)

axes[0].set_xlabel('Month')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Month')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

I notice that the summer months of June-September usually have the peak activity for the city of Chicago (like clockwork) and even for New Orleans! There are also some interesting poeaks for New Orleans that may possibly point to something more. Let's investigate.¶

Let's examine the number of reports under each request reason for the three trimesters of the year T1 (JAN-APR), T2 (MAY-AUG), and T3 (SEP-DEC).¶

Chicago Summer Peak Driving Factors¶

In [107]:
trimester_order = ['T1', 'T2', 'T3']

chi_trimester_request_table = (
    chi_311.assign(
        trimester=pd.cut(
            chi_311['date_created'].dt.month,
            bins=[0, 4, 8, 12],
            labels=['T1', 'T2', 'T3']
        )
    )
    .groupby(['request_reason', 'trimester'])
    .size()
    .unstack(fill_value=0)
    .reindex(columns=trimester_order, fill_value=0)
    .reset_index()
)



chi_trimester_request_table.head(109)

Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.

99 Vicious Animal Complaint 11711 13640 12766
100 Wage Complaint 357 528 487
101 Water Lead Test Kit Request 21227 24968 19440
102 Water Lead Test Visit Request 2687 3198 3185
103 Water On Street Complaint 18726 25702 11017
104 Water Quality Concern 1347 1564 1678
105 Water in Basement Complaint 9350 39923 9468
106 Weed Removal Request 419 89562 27839
107 Wire Basket Request 1743 3067 1986
108 Yard Waste Pick-Up Request 9488 34289 31850

For starters, we see a massive increase in reports for rodent, bat and wasp related issues moving from T1-T2. After winter months, animals and insects often come out of hibernation and are more visible. It also seems that there is a surgence in tree and weed cutting/removal requests in the second trimester of the year. This could be due to seasonal growth patterns, where trees and weeds grow more rapidly during the spring and summer months. Additionally, weather conditions such as increased rainfall and warmer temperatures can contribute to the proliferation of vegetation. We also see increase in incidents of flooded basements and streets in this T2 which corroborates with overgrowth of green spaces.¶

New Orleans Summer Peak Drivers¶

In [108]:
trimester_order = ['T1', 'T2', 'T3']

nola_trimester_request_table = (
    nola_311.assign(
        trimester=pd.cut(
            nola_311['date_created'].dt.month,
            bins=[0, 4, 8, 12],
            labels=['T1', 'T2', 'T3']
        )
    )
    .groupby(['request_reason', 'trimester'])
    .size()
    .unstack(fill_value=0)
    .reindex(columns=trimester_order, fill_value=0)
    .reset_index()
)

82 Non-Rodent Complaint (Mice, Possums, Snakes) 202 246 160
83 Non-Rodent Complaint (Possums & Snakes) 103 164 130
84 Occupational License Tax 146 112 59
85 Off Duty Officer for Hire 49 51 51
86 One Way Sign Missing or Damaged 37 34 64
87 Order Recycling Cart 6835 7712 10387
88 Order Second Trash Cart 1194 1127 1572
89 Order Trash Cart 6176 6847 6474
90 Park Maintenance/Clean Up 194 186 145
91 Parking Lane 28 35 42
92 Pay Property Tax 30 28 15
93 Personal Property Tax Question 69 56 30
94 Pothole 5522 6231 5254
95 Property Maintenance 10266 20470 11567
96 Push-up/Pavement Expansion 61 95 158
97 Repair Recycling Cart 2352 2517 2493
98 Repair Trash Cart 7333 8842 7744
99 Replace Recycling Cart 1478 1513 1438
100 Replace Trash Cart 7393 8949 8752
101 Report a Missed Trash/Recycling Pick Up 5 32 6
102 Request Grass Cutting Service (City Owned Prop... 172 733 297
103 Request Health Records (Immunization, Lead and... 62 75 40
104 Request Information on the Health Marketplace 51 75 47
105 Request Information on the Smoke Free Ordinance 5 1 2
106 Request Private Security Light 8 3 0
107 Request Tree Service (Right of Way/Public Prop... 2724 6246 5101
108 Request a Large Item Pick Up 27840 35230 28008

For New Orleans, we do not see any significant increase from one trimester to another. However it seems that requests for item/trash pickup increase in T2. We also see an increase in street and catch basin flooding related requests due to New Orleans receiving more rainfall in summer months [3].¶

New Orleans is unique due to its geography putting it inside area often impacted by hurricanes. The month-to-month chart for New Orleans has some interesting peaks that may be slightly out of place. I hypothesise that they are due to hurrican issues. Let's see!¶

Hurricane Ida Daily Trace (New Orleans, Late 2021)¶

In [83]:
ida_trace = (
    nola_311[nola_311['date_created'].between('2021-08-01', '2021-10-31 23:59:59')]
    .assign(day=lambda df: df['date_created'].dt.floor('D'))
    .groupby('day')
    .size()
    .reset_index(name='report_count')
)

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
sns.lineplot(data=ida_trace, x='day', y='report_count', color='#e76f51', linewidth=2)
plt.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2021-08-29'), color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, label='Hurricane Ida landfall')
plt.title('Daily New Orleans 311 Request Volume Around Hurricane Ida')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Number of Reports')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

My educated guess is, with the onset of IDA there was a drop in number of requests most likely due to severe weather warnings compelling people to leave the city, which followed by massive increase in number of daily requests as people returned to their homes.¶

Hurricane Francine Daily Trace (New Orleans, Late 2024)¶

In [85]:
francine_trace = (
    nola_311[nola_311['date_created'].between('2024-08-15', '2024-10-15 23:59:59')]
    .assign(day=lambda df: df['date_created'].dt.floor('D'))
    .groupby('day')
    .size()
    .reset_index(name='report_count')
)

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
sns.lineplot(data=francine_trace, x='day', y='report_count', color='#457b9d', linewidth=2)
plt.axvline(pd.Timestamp('2024-09-11'), color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, label='Hurricane Francine landfall')
plt.title('Daily New Orleans 311 Request Volume Around Hurricane Francine')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Number of Reports')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

For Francine, while there is a sharp peak, it occurs the next day of the hurricane impact day. Francine's impact was the greatest on the first day and weather went back to being okay the next day which corroborates with¶

Day-of-Week Activity Heatmap¶

Comparing when residents submit requests.¶

In [84]:
day_order = ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday', 'Sunday']
month_order = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun', 'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec']

nola_day_heatmap = (
    nola_311.assign(
        day_of_week=nola_311['date_created'].dt.day_name(),
        month=nola_311['date_created'].dt.strftime('%b')
    )
    .groupby(['day_of_week', 'month'])
    .size()
    .unstack(fill_value=0)
    .reindex(index=day_order, columns=month_order)
)

chi_day_heatmap = (
    chi_311.assign(
        day_of_week=chi_311['date_created'].dt.day_name(),
        month=chi_311['date_created'].dt.strftime('%b')
    )
    .groupby(['day_of_week', 'month'])
    .size()
    .unstack(fill_value=0)
    .reindex(index=day_order, columns=month_order)
)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6), sharey=True)
sns.heatmap(nola_day_heatmap, cmap='Blues', ax=axes[0], cbar=False)
axes[0].set_title('New Orleans Request Activity by Day and Month')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Month')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Day of Week')

sns.heatmap(chi_day_heatmap, cmap='Greens', ax=axes[1], cbar_kws={'label': 'Number of Reports'})
axes[1].set_title('Chicago Request Activity by Day and Month')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Month')
axes[1].set_ylabel('')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

It appears that more requests are received on the weekdays compared to the weekends. This heatmap also reaffirms how the second trimester gets a higher density of requests.¶

Categorical and Content Analysis¶

Top 10 Categories by City¶

Instead of standardizing categories immediately, this step lists the top 10 request categories for each city separately. This makes it easier to inspect the raw labels first and decide later which categories should be grouped together.¶

New Orleans Top 10 Request Types¶

In [113]:
nola_top_10 = nola_311['request_type'].value_counts().head(20).reset_index()
nola_top_10.columns = ['request_type', 'count']
nola_top_10
Out[113]:
request_type count
0 Trash/Recycling 307942
1 Abandoned Vehicles 46874
2 Property Maintenance 42158
3 Streetlights 31861
4 Roads and Streets 26191
5 Drainage 19303
6 Parks & Parkways 16250
7 Roads/Drainage 14651
8 Street and Traffic Signs 9604
9 Safety & Permits 8269
10 COVID-19 6963
11 Traffic Signals/Signs/Striping/Streetlights 6902
12 Traffic Signals 5919
13 Mosquito, Termite & Rodent Control 4420
14 General Services 4144
15 Health Related Request 3390
16 Utility Poles 1834
17 Tax and Revenue 1329
18 Healthy Homes 1150
19 Traffic Safety 963

Chicago Top 10 Request Reasons¶

In [114]:
chi_top_10 = chi_311['request_reason'].value_counts().head(20).reset_index()
chi_top_10.columns = ['request_reason', 'count']
chi_top_10
Out[114]:
request_reason count
0 311 INFORMATION ONLY CALL 4184158
1 Aircraft Noise Complaint 1918574
2 Graffiti Removal Request 576163
3 Rodent Baiting/Rat Complaint 308726
4 Garbage Cart Maintenance 303778
5 Pothole in Street Complaint 287700
6 Street Light Out Complaint 282239
7 Abandoned Vehicle Complaint 269189
8 Tree Trim Request (NO LONGER BEING ACCEPTED) 225630
9 Tree Debris Clean-Up Request 167420
10 Traffic Signal Out Complaint 145575
11 Building Violation 129510
12 Tree Emergency 124685
13 Blue Recycling Cart 119594
14 Weed Removal Request 117820
15 Sign Repair Request - All Other Signs 116383
16 Tree Removal Inspection 106462
17 Sanitation Code Violation 88689
18 Dead Animal Pick-Up Request 85728
19 Tree Planting Request 77800

While the raw number of reports from Chicago outnumber that of New Orleans, we can generally see that New Orleans top requests have to do with infrastructure issues pertaining to sanitation and roadworks. On the other hand, Chicago suffers from graffiti and rodent complaints which are not represnted in the top 10 requests in New Orleans.¶

Service Performance and Spatial Equity¶

Resolution Time Distribution¶

In [115]:
nola_resolved = nola_311[(nola_311['date_closed'].notna()) & (nola_311['date_closed'] >= nola_311['date_created'])].copy()
chi_resolved = chi_311[(chi_311['date_closed'].notna()) & (chi_311['date_closed'] >= chi_311['date_created'])].copy()

nola_resolved['time_to_close_days'] = (nola_resolved['date_closed'] - nola_resolved['date_created']).dt.total_seconds() / 86400
chi_resolved['time_to_close_days'] = (chi_resolved['date_closed'] - chi_resolved['date_created']).dt.total_seconds() / 86400

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5), sharey=True)

sns.histplot(nola_resolved['time_to_close_days'], bins=100, color='#2a9d8f', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('New Orleans Resolution Time Distribution')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Days to Close')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of Tickets')
axes[0].set_yscale('log')

sns.histplot(chi_resolved['time_to_close_days'], bins=100, color='#e76f51', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Chicago Resolution Time Distribution')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Days to Close')
axes[1].set_ylabel('')
axes[1].set_yscale('log')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Efficiency by District and Precinct¶

In [122]:
nola_district_efficiency = (
    nola_resolved[nola_resolved['council_district'].notna()]
    .groupby('council_district')['time_to_close_days']
    .median()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .reset_index()
)

chi_precinct_efficiency = (
    chi_resolved[chi_resolved['precinct'].notna()]
    .assign(precinct=pd.to_numeric(chi_resolved.loc[chi_resolved['precinct'].notna(), 'precinct'], errors='coerce'))
    .dropna(subset=['precinct'])
    .groupby('precinct')['time_to_close_days']
    .median()
    .reset_index()
)

chi_precinct_efficiency['precinct'] = chi_precinct_efficiency['precinct'].astype(int).astype(str)
precinct_order = sorted(chi_precinct_efficiency['precinct'].unique(), key=lambda x: int(x))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 10))

sns.barplot(
    data=nola_district_efficiency,
    x='time_to_close_days',
    y='council_district',
    hue='council_district',
    palette='crest',
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[0]
)
axes[0].set_title('Median Resolution Time by New Orleans Council District')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Median Days to Close')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Council District')

sns.barplot(
    data=chi_precinct_efficiency,
    x='time_to_close_days',
    y='precinct',
    order=precinct_order,
    hue='precinct',
    hue_order=precinct_order,
    palette='flare',
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[1]
)
axes[1].set_title('Median Resolution Time by Chicago Precinct')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Median Days to Close')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Precinct')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [124]:
nola_district_counts = (
    nola_311[nola_311['council_district'].notna()]
    .groupby('council_district')
    .size()
    .reset_index(name='request_count')
    .sort_values('request_count', ascending=False)
)

chi_precinct_counts = (
    chi_311[chi_311['precinct'].notna()]
    .assign(precinct=pd.to_numeric(chi_311.loc[chi_311['precinct'].notna(), 'precinct'], errors='coerce'))
    .dropna(subset=['precinct'])
    .groupby('precinct')
    .size()
    .reset_index(name='request_count')
)

chi_precinct_counts['precinct'] = chi_precinct_counts['precinct'].astype(int).astype(str)
precinct_order = sorted(chi_precinct_counts['precinct'].unique(), key=lambda x: int(x))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 10))

sns.barplot(
    data=nola_district_counts,
    x='request_count',
    y='council_district',
    hue='council_district',
    palette='crest',
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[0]
)
axes[0].set_title('Number of Requests by New Orleans Council District')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Number of Requests')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Council District')

sns.barplot(
    data=chi_precinct_counts,
    x='request_count',
    y='precinct',
    order=precinct_order,
    hue='precinct',
    hue_order=precinct_order,
    palette='flare',
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[1]
)
axes[1].set_title('Number of Requests by Chicago Precinct')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Number of Requests')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Precinct')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

For New Orleans, District E seems to be outperforming the other districts in median of days taken to close requests. However when looking at the area occupied by District E, it seems that most of it are natural reserves and only some part of it is habited. Rest of the districts seem on part with each other. District A seems to take the longest to close requests, upon inspection of area, it seems to occupy the largest chunk of residential New Orleans area.¶

It also makes sense that the requests marked Unknown take the longest time to close as they are likely moved around between districts and no one takes ownership of them.¶

I observe some anomalies in the Chicaho data. Two precincts seem to have a median resoution day of 0 while they receive the most requests out of all the precincts. This is a bit fishy. Let's investifate precinct 27 and 46!¶

In [137]:
target_precinct = 27

sub = chi_311[
    pd.to_numeric(chi_311['precinct'], errors='coerce') == target_precinct
].copy()

print('Total number of rows:', len(sub))

sub = chi_311[
    pd.to_numeric(chi_311['precinct'], errors='coerce') == target_precinct
].copy()

sub['date_created'] = pd.to_datetime(sub['date_created'], errors='coerce')
sub['date_closed'] = pd.to_datetime(sub['date_closed'], errors='coerce')

same_day_closed = sub[
    sub['date_closed'].notna() &
    (sub['date_closed'].dt.date == sub['date_created'].dt.date)
]

print(f'Precinct {target_precinct}')
print('number of complaints closed on the first day:', len(same_day_closed))
display(same_day_closed.head())

Total number of rows: 2042214
Precinct 27
number of complaints closed on the first day: 1949870
request_number request_reason origin date_created date_modified date_closed request_status responsible_agency address longitude latitude precinct month standardized_category
325 SR20-03452802 Building Violation Internet 2020-02-04 07:39:18 2020-02-04 09:13:51 2020-02-04 09:13:51 Completed DOB - Buildings 1335 W TOUHY AVE -87.664870 42.012259 27.0 2 Property and Buildings
3346 SR20-03489114 Dead Animal Pick-Up Request Phone Call 2020-02-10 07:36:50 2020-02-10 13:21:42 2020-02-10 13:21:42 Completed Streets and Sanitation 9300 S JEFFERY AVE -87.575486 41.726033 27.0 2 Sanitation
6767 SR20-03306952 Traffic Signal Out Complaint Phone Call 2020-01-12 09:55:01 2020-01-12 20:50:19 2020-01-12 20:50:19 Completed CDOT - Department of Transportation 5100 S LAKE PARK AVE -87.587872 41.802389 27.0 1 Lighting and Signals
7438 SR20-04008522 Sanitation Code Violation Phone Call 2020-04-29 06:24:48 2020-04-29 11:20:42 2020-04-29 11:20:42 Completed Streets and Sanitation 7242 S SACRAMENTO AVE -87.698117 41.761358 27.0 4 Other
8046 SR20-04395693 Traffic Signal Out Complaint Phone Call 2020-06-20 19:32:10 2020-06-20 19:40:58 2020-06-20 19:40:58 Canceled CDOT - Department of Transportation 1601 E 95TH ST -87.585133 41.722347 27.0 6 Lighting and Signals
In [139]:
target_precinct = 46

sub = chi_311[
    pd.to_numeric(chi_311['precinct'], errors='coerce') == target_precinct
].copy()

print('Total number of rows:', len(sub))

sub = chi_311[
    pd.to_numeric(chi_311['precinct'], errors='coerce') == target_precinct
].copy()

sub['date_created'] = pd.to_datetime(sub['date_created'], errors='coerce')
sub['date_closed'] = pd.to_datetime(sub['date_closed'], errors='coerce')

same_day_closed = sub[
    sub['date_closed'].notna() &
    (sub['date_closed'].dt.date == sub['date_created'].dt.date)
]

modified_other_day = sub[
    sub['date_closed'].notna() &
    (sub['date_closed'].dt.date != sub['date_modified'].dt.date)
]


print(f'Precinct {target_precinct}')
print('number of complaints closed on the first day:', len(same_day_closed))
display(same_day_closed.head())

print('number of complaints modified on a different day than closed:', len(modified_other_day))
display(modified_other_day.head())

Total number of rows: 4066974
Precinct 46
number of complaints closed on the first day: 4034799

13286 SR20-04206570 Tree Removal Inspection Mobile Device 2020-05-26 14:47:39 2020-07-01 00:05:59 2020-06-02 13:15:31 Completed Streets and Sanitation 6849 S KEDVALE AVE -87.725914 41.768076 46.0 5 Trees and Landscaping
13882 SR20-03368776 Snow – Uncleared Sidewalk Complaint Internet 2020-01-22 08:43:05 2020-06-30 21:54:48 2020-01-22 13:39:38 Completed CDOT - Department of Transportation 1518 N SEDGWICK ST -87.638850 41.909853 46.0 1 Roadwork
14190 SR20-04253296 Street Light Out Complaint Mobile Device 2020-06-02 08:02:58 2020-06-30 21:26:53 2020-06-03 00:59:14 Completed CDOT - Department of Transportation 5302 N NATCHEZ AVE -87.789319 41.977673 46.0 6 Roadwork

It seems that these two precincts seem to close most of their requests to the day those requests were first created. It is interesting to note that the date_modified for some of these is different than the date the request was actually closed. Perhaps precinct 27 and 46 indeed are highly eddicient.¶

Median vs. Mean Resolution Time by Category¶

In [118]:
nola_mean_median = (
    nola_resolved.groupby('request_reason')['time_to_close_days']
    .agg(['median', 'mean', 'count'])
    .sort_values('median', ascending=False)
    .head(15)
    .reset_index()
)

chi_mean_median = (
    chi_resolved.groupby('request_reason')['time_to_close_days']
    .agg(['median', 'mean', 'count'])
    .sort_values('median', ascending=False)
    .head(15)
    .reset_index()
)

nola_mean_median['city'] = 'New Orleans'
chi_mean_median['city'] = 'Chicago'

resolution_summary_table = pd.concat([nola_mean_median, chi_mean_median], ignore_index=True)
resolution_summary_table
Out[118]:
request_reason median mean count city
0 Hard Rock Event- Medical Needs 1650.674965 1650.674965 1 New Orleans
1 Hard Rock Event- Vehicle Pick Up 1594.699091 1594.699091 2 New Orleans
2 School Zone 1571.929931 1545.243865 19 New Orleans
3 Sanitation Supplies 1497.787303 1057.089100 9 New Orleans
4 COVID-19 Business Non-Compliance 1484.795810 1474.239387 2181 New Orleans
5 Hard Rock Event- Vehicle Retrieval 1421.995127 1421.995127 2 New Orleans
6 Request Health Records (Immunization, Lead and... 1410.830556 1215.870105 47 New Orleans

10 Construction permit 1279.833299 1006.312608 485 New Orleans
11 Request Information on the Health Marketplace 1095.796134 1037.837636 145 New Orleans

14 Lane Striping or other Pavement Markings 947.674398 872.335194 301 New Orleans
15 Sidewalk Inspection Request 707.345706 760.819194 32613 Chicago
16 Shared Cost Sidewalk Program Request 388.297025 470.899718 8724 Chicago
17 Street Light Pole Door Missing Complaint 303.973981 456.882598 4495 Chicago
18 Protected Bike Lane - Debris Removal 273.033021 310.382559 1245 Chicago
19 Tree Planting Request 234.719664 287.015038 55641 Chicago
20 Street Light On During Day Complaint 177.029381 220.377733 6120 Chicago
21 Tree Removal Inspection 121.770567 167.217333 97096 Chicago
22 Licensed Pharmaceutical Representative Complaint 104.494884 210.433343 47 Chicago
23 Viaduct Light Out Complaint 104.005446 178.829771 2372 Chicago
24 Buildings - Plumbing Violation 73.077251 81.897780 45018 Chicago
25 Water Lead Test Kit Request 59.275822 105.890202 28679 Chicago
26 Paid Sick Leave Violation 56.173362 188.502655 1326 Chicago
27 Consumer Fraud Complaint 54.917494 89.931984 7832 Chicago
28 Water Lead Test Visit Request 46.062436 82.139490 7482 Chicago
29 Wage Complaint 44.902650 121.978266 1215 Chicago

The top request types with high median resolution days for New Orleans is Covid business non-compliance and Licenses and permits. For the former, we can see how that may have fallen down the priority list.¶

For chicago the top high median request is sidewalk inspection. This can impact a large number of pedestrians especially those with disabilities which is why it's a shame that it is low priority. Other reasons are Tree related! Tree Removal inspection and Tree Planting are one of the top requests with high median, followed by plumbing violations.¶
